one_line_takeaway: Claude Code-based autonomous security code review can be reliably defeated (100% success, 17/17 real CVEs) by an attacker who iteratively refines adversarial PR metadata against a local clone of the same review pipeline, exploiting a fundamental test-refine asymmetry that gives defenders only one look at the final, polished attack.
key_finding: Exploratory study: strong bug-free framing caused large, mostly statistically significant (Bonferroni-corrected z-tests) drops in detection on vulnerable files -- GPT-4o-mini fell from 97.2% (240/247) to 3.6% (9/247), a 93.5pp decline; Claude 3.5 Haiku -59.9pp; DeepSeek V3 -42.9pp; Claude Sonnet 4.5 -16.7pp; Gemini 2.0 Flash -16.2pp; Claude Opus 4.5 was the only model with a non-significant drop (-4.9pp), making it the most resilient. All models also had very high false-positive rates on patched files even under neutral framing (>88%, except Claude 3.5 Haiku at 68.4%). Attack study: the unbiased baseline PR was already rejected in 16/17 cases (94%); template-based attacks flipped none of these rejections and sometimes backfired (the sole baseline-accepted case, CVE-2025-66416, was rejected under some template combinations that triggered suspicion). The novel LLM-assisted attack succeeded at a single attempt (Biased-1) in 9/17 cases (53%), and the iterative refinement loop (Biased-2) brought the remaining 8 cases to acceptance, for a cumulative 100% (17/17) success rate; 82% of cases (14/17) succeeded within two refinement iterations, at a total API cost of $19.36 across all 17 attacks. Redacting the PR description alone (Debiased-1) restored correct rejection in 12/17 cases (70%); adding an explicit instruction to ignore metadata (Debiased-2) recovered 4 more of the remaining 5, for an overall 16/17 (94%) restored detection rate -- with the sole exception (CVE-2025-66416) being a vulnerability that only affects a non-default, non-recommended deployment configuration.
core_contribution: The paper (titled in the PDF 'Measuring and Exploiting Contextual Bias in LLM-Assisted Security Code Review', by Mitropoulos, Alexopoulos, Alexopoulos, and Spinellis) makes three original contributions: (1) a controlled exploratory study measuring the framing effect on file-level vulnerability detection across 6 LLMs and 5 framing conditions (14,910 queries total), establishing that 'bug-free' framing (asserting code is secure) causes the largest detection drops; (2) a novel experimental testbed that emulates 10 real-world Automated Code Review (ACR) pipelines using Claude Code, evaluating vulnerability re-introduction attacks across 17 CVEs; and (3) two attack strategies against these live ACR pipelines -- a template-based attack (adapted from prior bias templates) and a novel LLM-assisted iterative refinement attack that the authors designed themselves, which achieves a 100% (17/17) success rate. They also evaluate and validate two debiasing defenses (metadata redaction, and redaction plus explicit instructions).
threat_model: A supply-chain attacker who has read access to a target project's public commit history and CVE/NVD records (a realistic assumption for OSS projects using GitHub-hosted, Claude Code-based autonomous ACR agents on pull requests). The adversary identifies a previously fixed vulnerability, reverts the fixing commit to reintroduce the vulnerable code, and crafts adversarial PR metadata (commit message, PR description, code comments) that frames the revert as benign or as a security improvement, aiming to get the ACR agent to recommend 'Approve' rather than 'Reject'. The key structural asymmetry the paper identifies is that the attacker can test and iteratively refine the adversarial PR against a private local clone of the target's actual review pipeline/prompt before submission, whereas the real ACR system only reviews the final, polished PR once.
limitations: Authors' own stated threats to validity: (1) Generalization -- results target Claude Code specifically as deployed via GitHub Actions (mostly Sonnet 4.5, three projects on Opus 4.5); transfer to other ACR tools (e.g., CodeRabbit, Greptile) or model configurations is untested and left to future work; the 17-CVE/10-project sample, while spanning 7 languages and a wide popularity range (314-71.1k stars), is limited in count due to the manual per-project setup burden. (2) Internal validity -- Claude Code was run locally rather than through live GitHub Actions to avoid touching production systems, so the review agent lacked GitHub-native context (issue trackers, prior PR discussion threads), partially mitigated by the agent's repository history access and web-search capability. (3) The attacker in the study uses the same LLM as the ACR pipeline being attacked; different attacker/defender LLM pairings are left for future work. (4) The paper does not test how contextual-bias injection fares against human reviewers, only noting a subjective judgment that the synthetic attacks would likely raise human suspicion. (5) Reverting a fixing commit does not guarantee the vulnerability is actually reintroduced (later defenses may have been added), though the authors argue this does not affect their bias-comparison-focused conclusions.

datasets_used: CrossVul (27,476 files / 13,738 vulnerable-fixed pairs; reduced to 3,968 single-file pairs within 100,000-token limit; stratified sample of 250 pairs / 497 files across 5 CWE-language combinations: CWE-79 PHP, CWE-79 JavaScript, CWE-89 PHP, CWE-125 C, CWE-787 C)

Self-constructed dataset of 17 real CVEs across 10 real-world GitHub projects using Claude Code ACR (314 to 71.1k GitHub stars; TypeScript, C++, Python, Go, Ruby, JavaScript, PHP): strapi, xbmc, cherry-studio, mlflow, shakapacker, local-deep-research, typebot.io, WordPress-Simple-History, whodb, MCP/python-sdk
quantitative_results: Exploratory study, strong bug-free framing detection drop on vulnerable code (Neutral -> Strong Bug-free): GPT-4o-mini 97.2%->3.6% (-93.5pp, p<0.001); Claude 3.5 Haiku -59.9pp (p<0.001); DeepSeek V3 96.8%->53.8% (-42.9pp, p<0.001); Claude Sonnet 4.5 97.4%->80.6% (-16.7pp, p<0.001); Gemini 2.0 Flash 95.5%->79.4% (-16.2pp, p<0.001); Claude Opus 4.5 95.3%->90.4% (-4.9pp, not significant)

False positive rate on patched files under neutral framing: >88% for GPT-4o-mini, Gemini 2.0 Flash, DeepSeek V3, Sonnet 4.5, Opus 4.5; 68.4%->31.6% Fixed rate for Claude 3.5 Haiku (i.e. Haiku's FPR 31.6% under neutral, the lowest of the six)
Manual validation of correct detections under neutral framing: incorrect (spurious) justification rate 71.0% (Claude 3.5 Haiku) to 57.6% (Gemini 2.0 Flash)

Template-based attack (RQ2): baseline 16/17 (94%) rejected; 0 of 136 template permutations (8 combos x 17 CVEs) flip any rejection to acceptance; sole baseline-accepted CVE (2025-66416, mcp/python-sdk) gets rejected under some template combinations

LLM-assisted attack (RQ3): Biased-1 (single seed prompt) succeeds in 9/17 (53%); iterative refinement (Biased-2) brings remaining 8 cases to acceptance for cumulative 17/17 (100%) success; 82% (14/17) succeed within 2 iterations; total API cost across all 17 attacks = $19.36 (cumulative cost by iteration: n=1 $11.53, n=2 $16.91, n=3 $18.41, n=4 $19.13, n=5 $19.36)

Debiasing: Debiased-1 (PR description redacted) restores correct rejection in 12/17 (70%) of previously successful-attack cases; Debiased-2 (redaction + explicit instruction to ignore metadata) recovers 4 more, giving overall 16/17 (94%) restored detection
baselines_compared: Neutral framing condition (no security framing) as the reference point for measuring framing-induced detection shifts in the exploratory study
Baseline (unbiased) PR with commit message/description 'Change {filenames}' as the reference attack condition (16/17 rejected) against which template-based and LLM-assisted attacks are compared

Template-based bias injection (adapted from Moon et al.'s bias-template taxonomy for LLM code-correctness judging) as a weaker attack baseline compared against the paper's own novel LLM-assisted iterative refinement attack
